question_text: Fusion processes, like combining two deuterons to form a He nucleus are impossible at ordinary temperatures and pressure. The reasons for this can be traced to the fact: nuclear forces have a short-range. nuclei are positively charged. the original nuclei must be completely ionized before fusion can take place. the original nuclei must first break up before combining with each other.
question_html:
Fusion processes, like combining two deuterons to form a He nucleus are impossible at ordinary temperatures and pressure. The reasons for this can be traced to the fact:

  1. nuclear forces have a short-range.
  2. nuclei are positively charged.
  3. the original nuclei must be completely ionized before fusion can take place.
  4. the original nuclei must first break up before combining with each other.
solution_text: Two deuterons can combine to form He atom when their nuclei come close to the nuclear range where electrostatic repulsive force between positively charged deuterons does not act. The electrostatic force increases very high on decreasing their distance {tex}\left(\because F \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}\right){/tex} To overcome this electrostatic repulsive force nuclei need very high temperature and pressure. Hence to combine two nuclei, they must reach closer to the range of where nuclear force acts and electrostatic repulsive force does not act verifies the answer (a) and (b).
solution_html:
Two deuterons can combine to form He atom when their nuclei come close to the nuclear range where electrostatic repulsive force between positively charged deuterons does not act. The electrostatic force increases very high on decreasing their distance {tex}\left(\because F \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}\right){/tex}
To overcome this electrostatic repulsive force nuclei need very high temperature and pressure. Hence to combine two nuclei, they must reach closer to the range of where nuclear force acts and electrostatic repulsive force does not act verifies the answer (a) and (b).

question_text: Which one of the following cannot emit radiation and why? Excited nucleus, excited electron.
question_html: Which one of the following cannot emit radiation and why? Excited nucleus, excited electron.
solution_text: The energy of the internal motion of a nucleus is quantized. A typical nucleus has a set of allowed energy levels, including a ground state (state of lowest energy and several excited states. Because of the great strength of nuclear interactions, excitation energies of nuclei are typical of the order of MeV, compared with a few eV for atomic energy levels. In ordinary physical and chemical transformations, the nucleus always remains in its ground state. When a nucleus is placed in an excited state, either by bombardment with high-energy particles or by a radioactive transformation, it can decay to the ground state by emission of one or more photons called gamma rays or gamma-ray photons, with typical energies of 10 keV to 5 MeV. This process is called gamma ( {tex}\gamma{/tex} ) decay. An excited nucleus can emit radiation, but excited electron cannot. The excited electron cannot emit radiation because the energy of electronic energy levels is in the range of eV and not in MeV, {tex}\gamma{/tex} -radiations have the energy of the order of MeV.

question_text: Why do stable nuclei never have more protons than neutrons?
question_html: Why do stable nuclei never have more protons than neutrons?
solution_text: If in a stable nucleus number of protons are larger than neutrons the repulsive electrostatic force between proton-proton becomes larger in place of nuclear force of attraction between nucleons. So for stability, repulsive force between proton-proton must be smaller than nuclear attractive force between nucleons.

question_html: A piece of wood from the ruins of an ancient building was found to have a 14C activity of 12 disintegrations per minute per gram of its carbon content. The 14C activity of the living wood is 16 disintegration per minute per gram. How long ago did the tree, from which the wooden sample came, die? Given the half-life of 14C is 5760 years.
solution_text: The rate of disintegration in an old wood sample of 14C radioactive atoms is 12 atoms per min gm. Initially, rate of disintegration of 14 C when the tree was live = 16 atoms per min per gm. {tex}T_{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} of 14 C nuclei = 5760 years According to radioactive decay law, {tex}\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}_{0} \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}}{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{R}_{0} \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}}{/tex} 12 = 16 {tex}\mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}}{/tex} {tex}e^{\lambda t}=\frac{16}{12}{/tex} {tex}\log _{e} e^{i t}=\log _{e} \frac{4}{3}{/tex} {tex}\lambda t=\log _{e} \frac{4}{3}{/tex} {tex}t=\frac{2.303 \times \log _{10}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)}{\lambda} \text { halflife }\left(\because \lambda=\frac{0.6931}{T_{12}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{2.303 \times(\log 4-\log 3) \times 5760}{0.6931}{/tex} {tex}\therefore t=\frac{2.303(0.6020-0.4771) 5760}{0.6931}{/tex} = 2391.20 years
solution_html:
The rate of disintegration in an old wood sample of 14C radioactive atoms is 12 atoms per min gm. Initially, rate of disintegration of 14C when the tree was live = 16 atoms per min per gm.
{tex}T_{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} of 14C nuclei = 5760 years
According to radioactive decay law,
{tex}\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}_{0} \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}}{/tex} or {tex}\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{R}_{0} \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}}{/tex}
12 = 16{tex}\mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}}{/tex}
{tex}e^{\lambda t}=\frac{16}{12}{/tex}
{tex}\log _{e} e^{i t}=\log _{e} \frac{4}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda t=\log _{e} \frac{4}{3}{/tex}
{tex}t=\frac{2.303 \times \log _{10}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)}{\lambda} \text { halflife }\left(\because \lambda=\frac{0.6931}{T_{12}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2.303 \times(\log 4-\log 3) \times 5760}{0.6931}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore t=\frac{2.303(0.6020-0.4771) 5760}{0.6931}{/tex} = 2391.20 years

question_text: Are the nucleons fundamental particles, or do they consist of still smaller parts? One way to find out is to probe a nucleon just as Rutherford probed an atom. What should be the kinetic energy of an electron for it to be able to probe a nucleon? Assume the diameter of a nucleon to be approximately 10 –15 m.
question_html: Are the nucleons fundamental particles, or do they consist of still smaller parts? One way to find out is to probe a nucleon just as Rutherford probed an atom. What should be the kinetic energy of an electron for it to be able to probe a nucleon? Assume the diameter of a nucleon to be approximately 10–15 m.
solution_text: Each particle (neutron and proton) present inside the nucleus is called a nucleon. Let λ be the wavelength λ=10 −15 m. To detect separate parts inside a nucleon the electron must have wavelength less than 10 −15 m. {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 10 -15 m {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}\because {/tex} {tex}E=h v=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} [ {tex}\because {/tex} c = v {tex}\lambda{/tex} ] K.E. = P.E. of (electron) = {tex}\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{10^{-15}}{/tex} J K.E. = {tex}\frac{6.6 \times 3 \times 10^{-34+8+15}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} eV = {tex}\frac{99 \times 10^{-34+23+19}}{8}{/tex} = 12.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34+42 = 1.24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 +8 K.E. = 1.24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 eV So the K.E. of particle which may detect nucleon inside the nucleus must be of 1.24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 eV per particle.

Let λ be the wavelength λ=10−15m.

question_text: A nuclide 1 is said to be the mirror isobar of nuclide 2 if Z 1 =N 2 and Z 2 =N 1 What nuclide is a mirror isobar of 11 Na 23 ? Which nuclide out of the two mirror isobars have greater binding energy and why?
question_html:
A nuclide 1 is said to be the mirror isobar of nuclide 2 if Z1 =N2 and Z2 =N1

  1. What nuclide is a mirror isobar of 11Na23?
  2. Which nuclide out of the two mirror isobars have greater binding energy and why?
solution_text: Here Z is atomic number and N is no. of neutron in 11 Na 23 Z 1 = 11 N 1 = 23 - 11 = 12 Mirror isobar of 11 Na 23 is Z 2 = N 1 = 12 So Mg is isobar of 11 Na 23 . So 12 Mg 23 is the mirror isobar of 11 Na 23 . As the neutrons in 12 Mg 23 are ‘11’ and in 11 Na 23 are ‘12’ so, the number of neutrons in Na is larger than Mg and hence nuclear short range attractive forces in Na will be larger than repulsive electrostatic forces between proton-proton. So, 11 Na 23 has more binding energy than 12 Mg 23 .
solution_html:
1. Here Z is atomic number and N is no. of neutron in 11Na23
    Z1 = 11
    N1 = 23 - 11 = 12
    Mirror isobar of 11Na23 is
    Z2 = N1 = 12
    So Mg is isobar of 11Na23.
    So 12Mg23 is the mirror isobar of 11Na23.
  2. As the neutrons in 12Mg23 are ‘11’ and in 11Na23 are ‘12’ so, the number of neutrons in Na is larger than Mg and hence nuclear short range attractive forces in Na will be larger than repulsive electrostatic forces between proton-proton.
    So, 11Na23 has more binding energy than 12Mg23.

question_text: Sometimes a radioactive nucleus decays into a nucleus which itself is radioactive. An example is: {tex}{}^{38}Sulphur\;\xrightarrow[{=2.48h}]{half-life}{}^{38}Cl\xrightarrow[{=0.62h}]{half-life}{}^{38}Ar\;(stable){/tex} Assume that we start with 1000 38 S nuclei at time t = 0. The number of 38 Cl is of count zero at t = 0 and will again be zero at t = {tex}\infty{/tex} . At what value of t, would the number of counts be a maximum?
question_html:
Sometimes a radioactive nucleus decays into a nucleus which itself is radioactive. An example is:
{tex}{}^{38}Sulphur\;\xrightarrow[{=2.48h}]{half-life}{}^{38}Cl\xrightarrow[{=0.62h}]{half-life}{}^{38}Ar\;(stable){/tex}
Assume that we start with 1000 38S nuclei at time t = 0. The number of 38Cl is of count zero at t = 0 and will again be zero at t = {tex}\infty{/tex}. At what value of t, would the number of counts be a maximum?
solution_text: According to the given question, we have 38 S {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 38 Cl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 38 Ar (stable), let N 1 and N 2 represent the numbers of active nuclei of 38 S and 38 Cl respectively at time t. If {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} are the disintegration constants for 38 S and 38 Cl, then {tex}\frac{d N_{1}}{d t}=-\lambda_{1} N_{1} {/tex} and {tex}\frac{d N_{2}}{d t}{/tex} = rate of decay of 38 Cl + rate of formation of 38 S, i.e. {tex}\frac{d N_{2}}{d t}=-\lambda_{2} N_{2}+\lambda_{1} N_{1}{/tex} (as rate of formation of 38 Cl from 38 S = - rate of decay of 38 S = {tex}\lambda_{1} N_{1}{/tex} ) Or {tex}e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t\left(\frac{d N_{2}}{d{t}}\right)=e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t\left(-\lambda_{2} N_{2}\right)+e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t\left(\lambda_{1} N_{1}\right){/tex} (multiplying both sides by {tex}e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t{/tex} ) Or {tex}e^{\lambda_{2} t} d N_{2}=-\lambda_{2} N_{2} e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t+\lambda_{1} e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t\left(N_{0} e^{-\lambda_{4} t}\right){/tex} {tex}\left(\text { as } N_{1}=N_{0} e^{-\lambda t}\right){/tex} Or {tex}e^{\lambda_{2} t} d N_{2}+\lambda_{2} N_{2} e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t=\lambda_{1} N_{0} e^{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right)} d t{/tex} ...(i) Integrating both sides of Eqn. (i), we get {tex}\int e^{\lambda_{2} t} d N_{2}+\lambda_{2} N_{2} \int e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t=\lambda_{1} N_{0} \int e^{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right) r} d t{/tex} {tex}\left(N_{2} e^{\lambda_{2} t}-N_{2} \lambda_{2} \int e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t\right)+\lambda_{2} N_{2} \int e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda_{1} N_{0}}{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right)} e^{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{4}\right) t}+C{/tex} where C is a constant of integration or {tex}N_{2} e^{\lambda_{2} t}=\frac{\lambda_{1} N_{0}}{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right)} e^{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right) t}+C{/tex} ...(ii) Since at t = 0, N 2 = 0, C = {tex}-\frac{\lambda_{1} N_{0}}{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right)}{/tex} ...(iii) Thus, from eqns. (ii) and (iii) we get , {tex}N_{2} e^{\lambda_{2} t}=\frac{\lambda_{1} N_{0}}{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right)}\left[e^{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right) t}-1\right]{/tex} or {tex}N_{2}=\frac{\lambda_{1} N_{0}}{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right)}\left(e^{-\lambda_{1} t}-e^{-\lambda_{2} t}\right){/tex} The count N 2 is maximum when {tex}\frac{d N_{2}}{d t}{/tex} = 0 or {tex}\frac{d}{d t}\left[e^{-\lambda_{1} t}-e^{-\lambda_{2} t}\right]{/tex} = 0 or {tex}e^{-\lambda_{1} t}\left(-\lambda_{1}\right)-e^{-\dot{\lambda}_{2} t}\left(-\lambda_{2}\right){/tex} = 0 or {tex}\frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\frac{\dot{e}^{-\lambda_{2} t}}{e^{-\lambda_{1} l}}=e^{\left(\lambda_{1}-\lambda_{2}\right) r}{/tex} or {tex}\left(\lambda_{1}-\lambda_{2}\right) t=\ln \left(\lambda_{1} / \lambda_{2}\right){/tex} or {tex}t=\frac{\ln \left(\lambda_{1} / \lambda_{2}\right)}{\left(\lambda_{1}-\lambda_{2}\right)}{/tex} Now, {tex}\lambda_{1}=\frac{\ln 2}{T_{1 / 2}(s)}{/tex} = 0.279 h -1 {tex}\lambda_{2} =\frac{\ln 2}{T_{1 / 2}(\mathrm{CI})}{/tex} = 1.118 h -1 t = {tex}\frac{\ln (0.279 / 1.118)}{0.279-1.118}{/tex} = 1.65 h ,these are the required results.
solution_html:
According to the given question, we have 38S {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 38Cl {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 38Ar (stable), let N1 and N2 represent the numbers of active nuclei of 38S and 38Cl respectively at time t. If {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} are the disintegration constants for 38S and 38Cl, then
{tex}\frac{d N_{1}}{d t}=-\lambda_{1} N_{1} {/tex} and
{tex}\frac{d N_{2}}{d t}{/tex} = rate of decay of 38Cl + rate of formation of 38S, i.e.
{tex}\frac{d N_{2}}{d t}=-\lambda_{2} N_{2}+\lambda_{1} N_{1}{/tex}
(as rate of formation of 38Cl from 38S = - rate of decay of 38S = {tex}\lambda_{1} N_{1}{/tex})
Or {tex}e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t\left(\frac{d N_{2}}{d{t}}\right)=e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t\left(-\lambda_{2} N_{2}\right)+e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t\left(\lambda_{1} N_{1}\right){/tex} (multiplying both sides by {tex}e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t{/tex})
Or {tex}e^{\lambda_{2} t} d N_{2}=-\lambda_{2} N_{2} e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t+\lambda_{1} e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t\left(N_{0} e^{-\lambda_{4} t}\right){/tex} {tex}\left(\text { as } N_{1}=N_{0} e^{-\lambda t}\right){/tex}
Or {tex}e^{\lambda_{2} t} d N_{2}+\lambda_{2} N_{2} e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t=\lambda_{1} N_{0} e^{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right)} d t{/tex} ...(i)
Integrating both sides of Eqn. (i), we get
{tex}\int e^{\lambda_{2} t} d N_{2}+\lambda_{2} N_{2} \int e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t=\lambda_{1} N_{0} \int e^{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right) r} d t{/tex}
{tex}\left(N_{2} e^{\lambda_{2} t}-N_{2} \lambda_{2} \int e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t\right)+\lambda_{2} N_{2} \int e^{\lambda_{2} t} d t{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\lambda_{1} N_{0}}{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right)} e^{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{4}\right) t}+C{/tex}
where C is a constant of integration
or {tex}N_{2} e^{\lambda_{2} t}=\frac{\lambda_{1} N_{0}}{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right)} e^{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right) t}+C{/tex} ...(ii)
Since at t = 0, N2 = 0, C = {tex}-\frac{\lambda_{1} N_{0}}{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right)}{/tex} ...(iii)
Thus, from eqns. (ii) and (iii) we get ,
{tex}N_{2} e^{\lambda_{2} t}=\frac{\lambda_{1} N_{0}}{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right)}\left[e^{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right) t}-1\right]{/tex}
or {tex}N_{2}=\frac{\lambda_{1} N_{0}}{\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right)}\left(e^{-\lambda_{1} t}-e^{-\lambda_{2} t}\right){/tex}
The count N2 is maximum when {tex}\frac{d N_{2}}{d t}{/tex} = 0
or {tex}\frac{d}{d t}\left[e^{-\lambda_{1} t}-e^{-\lambda_{2} t}\right]{/tex} = 0
or {tex}e^{-\lambda_{1} t}\left(-\lambda_{1}\right)-e^{-\dot{\lambda}_{2} t}\left(-\lambda_{2}\right){/tex} = 0
or {tex}\frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\frac{\dot{e}^{-\lambda_{2} t}}{e^{-\lambda_{1} l}}=e^{\left(\lambda_{1}-\lambda_{2}\right) r}{/tex}
or {tex}\left(\lambda_{1}-\lambda_{2}\right) t=\ln \left(\lambda_{1} / \lambda_{2}\right){/tex}
or {tex}t=\frac{\ln \left(\lambda_{1} / \lambda_{2}\right)}{\left(\lambda_{1}-\lambda_{2}\right)}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\lambda_{1}=\frac{\ln 2}{T_{1 / 2}(s)}{/tex} = 0.279 h-1
{tex}\lambda_{2} =\frac{\ln 2}{T_{1 / 2}(\mathrm{CI})}{/tex} = 1.118 h-1
t = {tex}\frac{\ln (0.279 / 1.118)}{0.279-1.118}{/tex} = 1.65 h ,these are the required results.

question_text: Deuteron is a bound state of a neutron and a proton with a binding energy B = 2.2 MeV. A {tex}\gamma{/tex} -ray of energy E is aimed at a deuteron nucleus to try to break it into a (neutron + proton) such that the n and p move in the direction of the incident {tex}\gamma{/tex} -ray. If E = B, show that this cannot happen. Hence calculate how much bigger than B must E be for such a process to happen.
question_html: Deuteron is a bound state of a neutron and a proton with a binding energy B = 2.2 MeV. A {tex}\gamma{/tex}-ray of energy E is aimed at a deuteron nucleus to try to break it into a (neutron + proton) such that the n and p move in the direction of the incident {tex}\gamma{/tex}-ray. If E = B, show that this cannot happen. Hence calculate how much bigger than B must E be for such a process to happen.
solution_text: Binding energy of a deuteron is, B = 2.2 MeV Let kinetic energy and the momentum of neutron and proton be K n , K p, and P n , P p respectively. From the conservation of energy, E - B = K n + K p = {tex}\frac{p_{n}^{2}}{2 m}+\frac{p_{p}^{2}}{2 m}{/tex} Now applying conservation of momentum is given by, p n + p p = {tex}\frac{E}{C}{/tex} As E = B, Eq. (i) {tex}p_{n}^{2}+p_{p}^{2}{/tex} = 0 It only happens if p n = p p So, the Eq. (ii) cannot be satisfied and the process cannot take place. Let us take E = B + x, where x << B for the process to take place. Putting the value of p n from Eq. (ii) in Eq. (i), we get or {tex}2p_{p}^{2}-\left(\frac{2 E}{c}\right) p_{p}+\left(\frac{E^{2}}{c^{2}}-2 m x\right){/tex} = 0 Solving the quadratic equation, we get p p = {tex}\frac{\frac{2 E}{c}+\sqrt{\frac{4 E^{2}}{c^{2}}-8\left(\frac{E^{2}}{c^{2}}-2 m x\right)}}{4}{/tex} For the real value p p , the discriminant is positive, Hence {tex}\frac{4 E^{2}}{c^{2}}=8\left(\frac{E^{2}}{c^{2}}-2 m x\right) {/tex} 16mx = {tex}\frac{4 E^{2}}{c^{2}} \Rightarrow x=\frac{E^{2}}{4 m c^{2}}{/tex} But x << B, hence E << B {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{B^{2}}{4 m c^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Binding energy of a deuteron is, B = 2.2 MeV Let kinetic energy and the momentum of neutron and proton be Kn, Kp, and Pn, Pp respectively. From the conservation of energy,
E - B = Kn + Kp = {tex}\frac{p_{n}^{2}}{2 m}+\frac{p_{p}^{2}}{2 m}{/tex}
Now applying conservation of momentum is given by,
pn + pp = {tex}\frac{E}{C}{/tex}
As E = B, Eq. (i) {tex}p_{n}^{2}+p_{p}^{2}{/tex} = 0
It only happens if pn = pp So, the Eq. (ii) cannot be satisfied and the process cannot take place.
Let us take E = B + x, where x << B for the process to take place. Putting the value of pn from Eq. (ii) in Eq. (i), we get
or {tex}2p_{p}^{2}-\left(\frac{2 E}{c}\right) p_{p}+\left(\frac{E^{2}}{c^{2}}-2 m x\right){/tex} = 0
Solving the quadratic equation, we get
pp = {tex}\frac{\frac{2 E}{c}+\sqrt{\frac{4 E^{2}}{c^{2}}-8\left(\frac{E^{2}}{c^{2}}-2 m x\right)}}{4}{/tex}
For the real value pp, the discriminant is positive, Hence
{tex}\frac{4 E^{2}}{c^{2}}=8\left(\frac{E^{2}}{c^{2}}-2 m x\right) {/tex}
16mx = {tex}\frac{4 E^{2}}{c^{2}} \Rightarrow x=\frac{E^{2}}{4 m c^{2}}{/tex}
But x << B, hence E << B
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{B^{2}}{4 m c^{2}}{/tex}

question_text: The deuteron is bound by nuclear forces just as H-atom is made up of p and e bound by electrostatic forces. If we consider the force between neutron and proton in deuteron as given in the form of a Coulomb potential but with an effective charge e ′ {tex}F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{e^{\prime 2}}{r}{/tex} estimate the value of ( e’/e ) given that the binding energy of a deuteron is 2.2 MeV.
question_html: The deuteron is bound by nuclear forces just as H-atom is made up of p and e bound by electrostatic forces. If we consider the force between neutron and proton in deuteron as given in the form of a Coulomb potential but with an effective charge e′{tex}F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{e^{\prime 2}}{r}{/tex} estimate the value of (e’/e) given that the binding energy of a deuteron is 2.2 MeV.
solution_text: The binding energy of H atom in ground state {tex}E=\frac{m e^{4}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2}}=13.6 e V{/tex} ...(i) If proton and neutron had charge e’ each and governed by the same electrostatic force, then in the above equation we would need to replace electronic mass m by the reduced mass m’ of proton-neutron (as some mass of proton and neutron is used by binding energy) and electronic charge e is replaced by e’ {tex}\frac{1}{m^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{M}+\frac{1}{N}{/tex} (M = mass of proton, mass of neutron) {tex}m^{\prime}=\frac{M N}{M+N}{/tex} (take M = N) = {tex}\frac{M}{2}{/tex} (If m = mass of proton) m' = {tex}\frac{1836 m}{2}{/tex} = 918 m {tex}\therefore{/tex} Binding energy {tex}(E)=\frac{918 m e^{t^{4}}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2}}{/tex} ....(ii) Dividing (ii) by (i) we get, {tex}\frac{E^{\prime}}{E}=\frac{918 e^{\prime} 4}{e^{4}}=\frac{2.2 M e V}{13.6 e V}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{e^{\prime}}{e}\right)^{4}=\frac{2.2 \times 10^{6} e V}{918 \times 13.6 e V}{/tex} {tex}\frac{e^{\prime}}{e}=\frac{2200000}{1248.48}=(176.21)^{14}{/tex} Required ratio {tex}\frac{e^{\prime}}{e}=3.64{/tex} .
solution_html:
The binding energy of H atom in ground state
{tex}E=\frac{m e^{4}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2}}=13.6 e V{/tex} ...(i)
If proton and neutron had charge e’ each and governed by the same electrostatic force, then in the above equation we would need to replace electronic mass m by the reduced mass m’ of proton-neutron (as some mass of proton and neutron is used by binding energy) and electronic charge e is replaced by e’
{tex}\frac{1}{m^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{M}+\frac{1}{N}{/tex} (M = mass of proton, mass of neutron)
{tex}m^{\prime}=\frac{M N}{M+N}{/tex} (take M = N)
= {tex}\frac{M}{2}{/tex} (If m = mass of proton)
m' = {tex}\frac{1836 m}{2}{/tex} = 918 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Binding energy {tex}(E)=\frac{918 m e^{t^{4}}}{8 \pi \varepsilon_{0}^{2} h^{2}}{/tex} ....(ii)
Dividing (ii) by (i) we get,
{tex}\frac{E^{\prime}}{E}=\frac{918 e^{\prime} 4}{e^{4}}=\frac{2.2 M e V}{13.6 e V}{/tex}
{tex}\left(\frac{e^{\prime}}{e}\right)^{4}=\frac{2.2 \times 10^{6} e V}{918 \times 13.6 e V}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{e^{\prime}}{e}=\frac{2200000}{1248.48}=(176.21)^{14}{/tex}
Required ratio {tex}\frac{e^{\prime}}{e}=3.64{/tex}.

question_text: Before the neutrino hypothesis, the beta decay process was thought to be the transition, n {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} p + {tex}\bar e{/tex} . If this was true, show that if the neutron was at rest, the proton and electron would emerge with fixed energies and calculate them. Experimentally, the electron energy was found to have a large range.
question_html: Before the neutrino hypothesis, the beta decay process was thought to be the transition, n {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} p + {tex}\bar e{/tex}. If this was true, show that if the neutron was at rest, the proton and electron would emerge with fixed energies and calculate them. Experimentally, the electron energy was found to have a large range.
solution_text: Let us consider the cases before and after {tex}\beta{/tex} -decay Before {tex}\beta{/tex} -decay; if the neutron was at rest. Hence, E n = m n c 2 , p n = 0 After {tex}\beta{/tex} -decay; p n = p p + p e or. 0 = p p + p e {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |p p | = |p e | = p.......(1) Also, energy of proton E p = {tex}(m_{p}^{2} c^{4}+p_{p}^{2} c^{2})^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} energy of electron E e = {tex}\left(m_{e}^{2} c^{4}+p_{p}^{2} c^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}=\left(m_{e}^{2} c^{4}+p_{e}^{2} c^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} from (1) st we get , Now applying conservation of energy, {tex}\left(m_{p}^{2} c^{4}+p^{2} c^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}=\left(m_{e}^{2} c^{4}+p^{2} c^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}=m_{n} c^{2}{/tex} m p c 2 = 936 MeV, m n c 2 = 938 MeV and m e c 2 = 0.51 MeV since, the energy difference between n and p is small, pc will be small, pc < < < m p c 2 , while pc may be greater than m e c 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad m_{p} c^{2}+\frac{p^{2} c^{2}}{2 m_{p}^{2} c^{4}}{/tex} = m n c 2 − pc To first order pc = m n c 2 - m p c 2 = 938 MeV - 936 MeV = 2 MeV This gives the momentum of proton or neutron. Then, E p = {tex}\left(m_{p}^{2} c^{4}+p^{2} c^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}=\sqrt{936^{2}+2^{2}}={/tex} 936 MeV E e = {tex}\left(m_{e}^{2} c^{4}+p^{2} c^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}=\sqrt{(0.51)^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex} = 2.06 MeV
solution_html:
Let us consider the cases before and after {tex}\beta{/tex} -decay
Before {tex}\beta{/tex}-decay; if the neutron was at rest. Hence, En = mnc2, pn = 0
After {tex}\beta{/tex}-decay; pn = pp + pe

energy of electron Ee = {tex}\left(m_{e}^{2} c^{4}+p_{p}^{2} c^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}=\left(m_{e}^{2} c^{4}+p_{e}^{2} c^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}  from (1) st we get ,
Now applying conservation of energy,

mpc2 = 936 MeV, mnc2 = 938 MeV and mec2 = 0.51 MeV
since, the energy difference between n and p is small, pc will be small, pc < < < mpc2, while pc may be greater than mec2
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad m_{p} c^{2}+\frac{p^{2} c^{2}}{2 m_{p}^{2} c^{4}}{/tex}  = mnc2 − pc
To first order pc = mnc2 - mpc2 = 938 MeV - 936 MeV = 2 MeV
This gives the momentum of proton or neutron. Then,
Ep = {tex}\left(m_{p}^{2} c^{4}+p^{2} c^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}=\sqrt{936^{2}+2^{2}}={/tex} 936 MeV
Ee = {tex}\left(m_{e}^{2} c^{4}+p^{2} c^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}=\sqrt{(0.51)^{2}+2^{2}}{/tex} = 2.06 MeV

question_text: Nuclei with magic no. of proton Z = 2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 52 and magic no. of neutrons N = 2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 82 and 126 are found to be very stable. Verify this by calculating the proton separation energy S p for 120 Sn (Z = 50) and 121 Sb = (Z = 51). The proton separation energy for a nuclide is the minimum energy required to separate the least tightly bound proton from a nucleus of that nuclide. It is given by S p = (M Z–1 , N + M H – M ZN ) c 2 . Given 119 In = 118.9058u, 120 Sn = 119.902199u, 121 Sb = 120.903824u, 1 H = 1.0078252u. What does the existence of magic number indicate?
question_html:
Nuclei with magic no. of proton Z = 2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 52 and magic no. of neutrons N = 2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 82 and 126 are found to be very stable.

  1. Verify this by calculating the proton separation energy Sp for 120Sn (Z = 50) and 121Sb = (Z = 51).
    The proton separation energy for a nuclide is the minimum energy required to separate the least tightly bound proton from a nucleus of that nuclide. It is given by Sp = (MZ–1, N + MH – MZN) c2.
    Given 119In = 118.9058u, 120Sn = 119.902199u, 121Sb = 120.903824u, 1H = 1.0078252u.
  2. What does the existence of magic number indicate?
solution_text: s p = [M z-1, n + M H - M z,N ] c 2 Here in this formula M z -1 is the mass of atom of Z-1 atomic number. M z is the mass of atom of mass number Z. {tex}\therefore {/tex} M z-1 = Mass of atom whose atomic number is 50 - 1 = 49 It is 49 ln 119 in this case m Z-1 = 19 ln 19 = 118.9058 and N = 119 - 49 = 70 s p for 50 Sn 120 = c 2 [118.9058 + 1.0078252 - 199.902199] s p for 50 Sn 120 = 0.0114362 c 2 Now for s p of 51 Sb 121 s p = [M z-1 , N +M H -M z , N] c 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Z = 51, Z - 1 = 50 for S n M z-1 = mass of 50 Sn = 199.902199 u {tex}\therefore{/tex} s p for 51 Sb 121 = [199.902199 + 1.0078252 - 120.903824] c 2 = 0.0059912c 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} S p ( 50 Sn 120 ) > S p ( 51 Sb 121 ) The existence of magic numbers indicates that the shell structure of nucleus is similar to the shell structure of atom. This also explains the peaks in binding energy per nucleon curve.
